title: First 30 minutes after an unfamiliar emergency
summary: Use a simple order—life safety, official information, accountability, utilities, and one next decision—when the exact hazard is still unclear.
content:
Use a simple order—life safety, official information, accountability, utilities, and one next decision—when the exact hazard is still unclear.

Action sequence: Move away from immediate fire, smoke, traffic, falling objects, floodwater, live electricity, and unstable structures. Call 112 for immediate danger and state what happened, exact location, people affected, and hazards still present. Account for household members without entering danger; send one short status message to the agreed outside contact. Follow official instructions, control only clearly safe utilities, and write the next review time. Safety boundary: Do not spend the first minutes collecting property while people remain exposed. Do not act on forwarded messages unless their source and time can be verified.
materials: flashlight. Context: “First 30 minutes after an unfamiliar emergency”.
steps: Move away from immediate fire, smoke, traffic, falling objects, floodwater, live electricity, and unstable structures.
Call 112 for immediate danger and state what happened, exact location, people affected, and hazards still present.
Account for household members without entering danger; send one short status message to the agreed outside contact.
Follow official instructions, control only clearly safe utilities, and write the next review time.
warnings: Do not spend the first minutes collecting property while people remain exposed.

alternatives: If “phone or radio” is unavailable, use a clean, clearly labelled substitute only when it serves the same safety function.

The safest reduced starting action for “First 30 minutes after an unfamiliar emergency” is: “Move away from immediate fire, smoke, traffic, falling objects, floodwater, live electricity, and unstable structures.”.

when_not_to_use: Do not use “First 30 minutes after an unfamiliar emergency” as a substitute for 112, evacuation orders, poison-centre advice, medical care, or qualified utility and structural assessment.

Do not continue when the scene exposes people to fire, smoke, floodwater, live electricity, gas, violence, unstable structures, or another uncontrolled hazard. Topic-specific stop check for “First 30 minutes after an unfamiliar emergency”: also follow this warning: “Do not spend the first minutes collecting property while people remain exposed.”.

title: One-minute 112 information card
summary: Prepare the few facts an emergency operator needs so stress, language barriers, or weak signal do not erase critical information.
content:
Prepare the few facts an emergency operator needs so stress, language barriers, or weak signal do not erase critical information.

Action sequence: Write the exact location, municipality, nearest landmark, floor, entrance, and access obstacle. Add what happened, how many people are affected, whether anyone is trapped, and whether fire, gas, electricity, water, traffic, or violence is present. List the caller name, callback number, language, and any deaf, speech, mobility, or cognitive communication need. Keep the line available, answer questions briefly, and follow the operator’s instructions. Safety boundary: Do not delay the call while trying to create a perfect report. Do not hang up or move to a different location unless safety or the operator requires it.
steps: Write the exact location, municipality, nearest landmark, floor, entrance, and access obstacle.

title: Immediate household accountability board
summary: Track who is safe, missing, injured, evacuated, or assigned a task without relying on memory or repeated calls.
content:
Track who is safe, missing, injured, evacuated, or assigned a task without relying on memory or repeated calls.

Action sequence: Create one row per person and animal with last confirmed time, place, condition, and next contact attempt. Mark information as confirmed, reported, or unknown; never convert an assumption into a fact. Assign one person to update the board and one alternate if that person leaves. Share only the minimum necessary information with responders and the outside contact. Safety boundary: Do not send untrained people into danger to resolve an unknown status. Do not publicly post names, medical details, or live locations without a genuine need.

title: Emergency stop-and-reassess trigger
summary: Define visible changes that automatically stop the current task before tunnel vision turns a manageable incident into a second emergency.
content:
Define visible changes that automatically stop the current task before tunnel vision turns a manageable incident into a second emergency.

Action sequence: Choose a universal stop word understood by the whole group. Stop immediately for smoke, gas odour, rising water, falling debris, live wires, violence, breathing difficulty, collapse, confusion, or a changed official instruction. Move to the preselected safe point and count everyone before discussing the next action. Record what triggered the stop and who authorizes any restart. Safety boundary: A stop signal is not optional and must not be punished or mocked. Do not restart merely because the original task feels unfinished.

title: Emergency pocket status message
summary: Use a compact message format that works by SMS and avoids long calls when networks are congested.
content:
Use a compact message format that works by SMS and avoids long calls when networks are congested.

Action sequence: Write: NAME – SAFE/NEED HELP – LOCATION – TIME – NEXT PLAN. Add one exact request only when needed, such as medicine pickup, transport, or confirmation of a meeting point. Send to the agreed outside contact, then preserve battery and wait for acknowledgement. Update only when condition, location, or plan changes. Safety boundary: Do not include rumours or unnecessary private medical details. A status message does not replace calling 112 for immediate danger.
